critic_raw:
Issue 1 — Site-Camera-Label Confound
The study combines four clinics with fundamentally different camera hardware, patient risk profiles, and label-source compositions. Two suburban clinics use newer polarized cameras and contribute most benign lesions. The academic center uses older cameras with different color response and contributes most confirmed melanomas. This means site, camera type, and malignancy label are collinear: a model can achieve high AUROC by learning camera and site signature rather than lesion morphology. The authors note strong color augmentation and resolution standardization, but neither addresses the systematic camera-site-label correlation — augmentation addresses within-image color variation, not the systematic difference between camera models. Evidence needed: site-stratified AUROC; analysis of whether site or camera type is independently predictive of malignancy after controlling for lesion appearance features.

Issue 2 — Referral Mix Shift During Rollout
During the prospective rollout period, the academic center expanded its biopsy triage clinic, increasing the share of difficult high-risk cases photographed there. This shifts the positive base rate upward in the rollout set at the academic center. If the model's 7-point AUROC advantage is measured on the prospective set that includes this expanded high-risk referral mix, part of the improvement may reflect a more favorable positive/negative ratio rather than better discrimination. No analysis is shown of whether the rollout set's case mix is comparable to the training set's case mix across sites. Evidence needed: site-stratified base rates in training vs. rollout period; stratified AUC showing whether improvement persists within each site independently.

Issue 3 — No Site-Stratified Evaluation
The prospective set is evaluated as a pooled sample with no site-stratified results and no camera-matched analysis. Given the structural confound described in Issue 1, the pooled AUROC is uninterpretable as evidence of generalization across sites with different cameras. A model that performs at chance at two of four sites but perfectly at the academic center could report strong pooled AUROC. Evidence needed: per-site AUROC for both old and new models; confusion matrix analysis by camera type.
defender_raw:
Pass 1 — Analysis:
Issue 1: This is the central concern. The camera-site-label correlation is explicitly noted in the case description: two suburban clinics contribute most benign lesions with new cameras; the academic center contributes most melanomas with old cameras. The color augmentation argument the authors make is addressed to within-image variation, not the systematic between-camera color response difference. The defender cannot credibly argue that color augmentation resolves a structural confound between camera model and label prevalence.

Issue 2: The biopsy triage expansion during rollout is a real base rate shift. If the rollout set has a higher proportion of high-risk academic-center cases than the training set, the apparent improvement may partly reflect a more favorable evaluation distribution for a model already biased toward the academic center's signal.

Issue 3: Without site-stratified evaluation, Issues 1 and 2 cannot be decomposed. This is the analytic gap that makes both other issues unresolvable without additional analysis.

Pass 2 — Verdicts:
Issue 1: Concede — color augmentation does not address systematic camera-site-label correlation.
Issue 2: Concede — referral mix shift during rollout is not analyzed.
Issue 3: Concede — no site-stratified evaluation.
Overall: critique_wins.
adjudication_raw:
ADJUDICATION — hidden_confounding_skin_sites_205 — Isolated Debate Run 1

All three issues conceded by defender. The structural confound is clear: camera type, site, and malignancy label are correlated, and the authors' mitigation (color augmentation) does not address this correlation. The biopsy triage expansion during rollout adds a second confound. The absence of site-stratified analysis means neither confound can be assessed from the presented data.

Verdict: CRITIQUE_WINS
